summary: The main claim is: Interleaving In human learning, it's not just what you learn but how you learn it that counts.
This post is a transcript of a recorded discussion, so it doesn't argue a single main claim — open the original to read the conversation.
The main claim is: A popular idea think this means that AGI would invent and use new technology such as nanorobotics to defeat us (this is the example Yudkowsky usually gives).

top_comment.author: p.b.
top_comment.text:
I think this extrapolates far from one example and I'm not sure the example applies all that well. 

Old engines played ugly moves because of their limitations, not because playing ugly moves is a super power. They won anyway because humans cannot out calculate engines. 

AlphaZero plays beautiful games and even todays standard engines don't play ugly or dumb looking moves anymore. I think in the limit superior play will tend to be beautiful and elegant. 

If there is a parallel between early super human chess and AGI takeover it will be that AGI uses less than brillant strategies that still work because of flawless or at least vastly superhuman execution. But these strategies will not look dump or incomprehensible.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: I think this extrapolates far from one example and I'm not sure the example applies all that well.

The main claim is: A non-expert human: Someone who can’t solve the task on their own, but will try to solve it using the assistant and some scalable alignment strategy.

The main claim is: Don't build AI + The AI you don't build is vacuously aligned.
In fact, it's probably right there in the original Friendly AI writings and I've just forgotten where.
The main claim is: Assuming outer alignment, inner alignment problems can occur only as the result of imperfect optimization.
But assuming that the deceptive inner optimizer is part of the outer-optimal policy, the outer objective must be unaligned.

The main claim is: 10% chance by 2045 50% chance by 2050 90% chance by 2070 Now that it’s 2020, I’m updating my forecast to: 10% chance by 2035 50% chance by 2045 90% chance by 2070 The main underlying shifts: more focus on improvements in tools, compute, an…
The main claim is: Let’s consider the case where the principals provide demonstrations, and get reward for those demonstrations .

The key insight of this paper is that applying a deep IRL algorithm here would be inefficient because it has to implicitly learn the unknown feature, and we can do much better by explicitly querying the human for the unknown feature.
